summary: Reviews the present perfect tense with haber, then introduces the imperfect (line in the past) for -ar verbs using the -aba ending. Teaches how to express ongoing or habitual past actions and combines haber with the conditional to form 'would have' structures.

teachings.summary: Reviewed the present perfect formed with haber + past participle (-ado for -ar verbs, -ido for -er/-ir verbs). The past participle never changes regardless of subject.
teachings.rule: Haber conjugated + -ado (for -ar verbs) or -ido (for -er/-ir verbs). The participle is invariable after haber.

examples.english: I have been able to eat
I haven't been able to eat

teachings.notes: The participle after haber never changes - he bailado, han bailado, hemos bailado all use bailado.

teachings.summary: Combining haber in the conditional (habría) with the past participle to form 'would have' structures. Habría is irregular - the e of haber falls out naturally.
teachings.rule: Habría + past participle (-ado/-ido). The e in habería naturally contracts to habría.

examples.english: I would have been able to go out
I wouldn't have been able to go out

teachings.notes: The contraction from habería to habría happens naturally through frequent use. Only a few verbs have this contraction.

teachings.summary: Introduced the imperfect tense as the 'line in the past' representing ongoing, habitual, or repeated past actions (I was doing, I used to do). For -ar verbs, remove -ar and add -aba as the base ending.
teachings.rule: For -ar verbs: remove -ar, add -aba (I/he/she/it/usted), -abas (tú), -ábamos (we), -aban (they/ustedes). The accent stays on -aba throughout.

examples.english: I was speaking / I used to speak
I was waiting / I used to wait
We were waiting for you (formal, masc)
They were trying to find it for me

teachings.notes: Three pasts in Spanish: (1) present perfect (he comido), (2) imperfect/line (comía), (3) preterite/dot (comí). The -aba form covers I/he/she/it/usted - same form for all.

teachings.summary: Introduced the framework of three pasts in Spanish: the 'I have eaten' past (present perfect), the 'line in the past' (imperfect - ongoing/habitual), and the 'dot in the past' (preterite - completed action).
teachings.rule: Line = I was eating, I used to eat, I would eat (habitual). Dot = I ate (completed). Have = I have eaten.
teachings.notes: All English past expressions fit into one of these three Spanish forms.
